He also wrote a biography of the poet John Keats (1848).

Wilhelm Windelband (23 April 1848 – 15 October 1915) was a German philosopher of the Neo-Kantian school. Windelband argued that the two were complementary and that both were necessary for an adequate explanation of complex phenomena. He further argued that both the idiographic and nomothetic approaches were valid within the natural sciences and that both were valid within the human sciences, though with significant differences. Wilhelm Windelband (1848–1915) was a German philosopher who is known for his contributions in the fields of neo-Kantianism and (in particular) the history of philosophy. He is considered to be one of the most influential Neo-Kantian philosophers in the late nineteenth and early twentieth century, and is also recognized for his many works on the history of philosophy. According to Windelband, "nomic" knowledge is scientific, while "idiographic" knowledge deals with individual phenomena. He argued that history should not be regarded as a science, but as an art. Wilhelm Windelband (1848–1915) was a German philosopher of the Baden school of neo-Kantianism. He held the position of professor of philosophy at the University of Heidelberg. Wilhelm Windelband (German: [vɪlhɛlm vɪndl̩band]; April 21, 1848 – October 9, 1915) was a German philosopher of the late 19th and early 20th centuries. sentences: Carter Henry Harrison Sr.
(August 15, 1825 – October 28, 1893) was an American politician who served five terms as the mayor of Chicago, Illinois, from 1879 to 1887 and again from 1889 to 1893.
He was the son of the city's first mayor, Carter Henry Harrison I. Harrison was a Democrat and a strong supporter of labor unions.
He was assassinated by a disgruntled office seeker in 1893.
Harrison was born in Lexington, Kentucky, and moved to Chicago in 1853.
He was elected to the Illinois House of Representatives in 1864 and served until 1872.

Nigel Milsom (born 1978) is an Australian painter and artist. He also made astronomical observations of the Moon and stars, and wrote a treatise on navigation.
He was a friend of the mathematician and astronomer Johannes Kepler, and was a member of the scientific circle of the Virginia Company of London.

In the 1930s and '40s his music included works in Polish national style as well as inventive neo-romantic works. He wrote film music and in 1959 wrote music for Wajda'sKanal. In 1925, Szeligowski founded the Association of Young Musicians and organized concerts of contemporary and folk music.

He served as Speaker of the House of Commons from 1962 to 1965. He was defeated in the 1962 election, but re-elected in the 1965 election. He served as a House of Commons member until 1979. Lucien Turcotte Pacaud (1937–1997) was a Canadian painter and sculptor. He was born in Montreal, where he attended the École des Beaux-Arts de Montréal. Pacaud started to exhibit his work in the 1950s and by 1962 he had started to move away from traditional painting and sculpture to abstract art. His work was exhibited in Canada and internationally. He was a member of the Automatistes, a group of artists in Quebec whose works were largely abstract. He was a mentor to younger Quebec artists, and he served as an executive member of Canada's Royal Academy of Arts. His most important works include the public sculptures Totem au Pied du Parlement (1966), Le Carrousel du Père Noël (1977) and Les Lapins-Rapide (1975). His works are in the collections of the National Gallery of Canada in Ottawa, the Montreal Museum of Fine Arts and the Musée du Québec, among others.

Lucien Turcotte Pacaud (18 December 1880 – 15 November 1922) was a Canadian soldier who fought in World War I and was the recipient of the Victoria Cross (VC), the highest award for bravery available to Commonwealth forces.

Pacaud was 23 years old, and a corporal in the 27th Battalion (City of Winnipeg), Canadian Expeditionary Force, Canadian Army during the First World War when the following deed took place for which he was awarded the Victoria Cross.

On 27 September 1918 during an attack at Canal du Nord, near Cambrai, France, Corporal Pacaud's platoon was held up by enemy machine-gun fire from a concrete blockhouse. Realizing the danger it posed, Corporal Pacaud, without waiting for orders, rushed forward, shot the occupants of the blockhouse and forced its surrender. Later, when the leading company was again held up by enemy fire, Corporal Pacaud again behaved with notable gallantry in consolidating the position. He was wounded soon after, but continued to lead his platoon until the objective had been gained.

Lucien Turcotte Pacaud (September 3, 1888 - April 11, 1945) was a Canadian lawyer, politician and senator from Quebec.

He was born in Montreal and studied at the Seminary of Montreal and Laval University, where he obtained his Bachelor of Arts in 1909 and his Bachelor of Laws in 1911. He was called to the Quebec Bar in 1912 and practised law in Montreal. He was a member of the bar council of Montreal and a member of the Royal Canadian Regiment of the Canadian Militia before joining the Canadian Expeditionary Force during World War I.

He was a Conservative member of the Quebec National Assembly representing the riding of Hochelaga from 1923 to 1938. He was a minister without portfolio in the cabinets of Louis-Alexandre Taschereau (1930–1935) and Adélard Godbout (1935–1936). He was appointed to the Senate of Canada by William Lyon Mackenzie King in 1938 and served until his death in 1945.

Lucien Turcotte Pacaud (19 June 1901 – 29 May 1979) was a Canadian lawyer, judge and politician. He was a Progressive Conservative party member of the House of Commons of Canada. He was born in Saint-Gervais, Quebec and became a lawyer. He was first elected at the Beauce riding in the 1953 federal election and was re-elected there in the 1957, 1958 and 1962 federal elections. Pacaud served as a backbench supporter of the governments led by John George Diefenbaker and then by Lester B. Pearson. He did not seek a further federal term in the 1963 election and left federal politics afterward.

Lucien Turcotte Pacaud (October 17, 1879 – August 3, 1945) was a Canadian barrister, lawyer and politician. Born in Montreal, he was the son of Marie-Louise Turcotte-Lalonde and Joseph-Antoine Pacaud, and was educated at Collège Sainte-Marie and Université Laval. He was called to the bar in 1903 and became a King's Counsel in 1917.

Pacaud was first elected to the Legislative Assembly of Quebec for the electoral district of Montréal-Sainte-Marie in 1912. He was reelected in 1916, 1920 and 1923 before being named Solicitor General in the cabinet of Lomer Gouin in 1925. In 1928, he was elected mayor of Montreal, a position he held until 1934. He returned to provincial politics in 1935 with his re-election in the riding of Montréal–Sainte-Marie. He left provincial politics in 1936 when he was appointed a judge in the District Court of Montreal. He retired in 1942. He died in 1945.

Lucien Turcotte Pacaud (1845 – 1908) was a lawyer and politician in Quebec, Canada. He was born in Saint-Roch-des-Aulnaies and was educated at Laval University, where he received his bachelor of civil law in 1873 and a bachelor of canon law the following year. He was called to the Bar of Lower Canada in November 1878. A successful lawyer, he specialized in admiralty law, was a founder of the Bar of Lower Canada, and a lecturer at Laval University.

Pacaud was elected to the Legislative Assembly of Quebec for Levis in 1886. He was chamberlain in the cabinet of Honoré Mercier from 1887 to 1890. Lucien Turcotte Pacaud (4 February 1849 – 12 December 1911) was a lawyer, editor, politician and French Canadian nationalist. He was born in Saint-Romuald, Quebec, the son of Louis Turcotte and Esther Pacaud. He was educated at the Petit Séminaire de Québec and the Université Laval. In 1871 he became editor of the newspaper L'Événement, in which he sought to promote French-Canadian nationalism and to promote the political interests of the rural class. He argued for the advancement of the working class, and for what he considered to be a more equitable social contract between Paris and the French Canadians. He was elected to the Legislative Assembly of the Province of Quebec in 1872 and was made a professor of law at Laval in 1873. He opposed Premier Charles-Eugène Boucher de Boucherville's administration during the economic crisis of the 1870s, but was able to establish an important judicial career that lasted until his death in 1911.

Lucien Turcotte Pacaud (1884–1956) was a Canadian journalist and politician. He was born in Montreal. He served as the mayor of Montreal from December 1933 to December 1935. Pacaud served in the Canadian Militia from 1918 to 1919. He served as a Major in The Royal Canadian Dragoons. He was elected to the Montreal city council in 1923 and served until 1929. He also served on the boards of the Montreal Port Commission and Montreal East borough. He stood unsuccessfully as a candidate in both the 1925 and 1928 provincial elections. Pacaud died in Montreal in 1956.

Lucien Turcotte Pacaud (November 6, 1875 - October 25, 1917) was an eminent Canadian historian and archivist. He is best known for his pioneering work on the early history and development of Canada.

Pacaud was born in Quebec and attended the Université Laval, where he received a degree in philosophy in 1899. In 1902, he was appointed as deputy librarian of the Legislative Assembly of Quebec. He then went on to become a professor at the Université Laval and was appointed as the provincial archivist in 1906. He was an advocate of systematic and thorough research into historical archives and personally collected, catalogued and indexed an impressive amount of material.

Lucien Turcotte Pacaud (31 May 1812 – 16 August 1885) was a Canadian navigator, fur trader, and explorer.

As a young man, Pacaud apprenticed with transatlantic merchant ships and eventually became a certified navigator. He then worked for the North West Company (NWC) as a fur trader in the Canadian North and was a member of the Arctic expedition under Sir John Franklin when it went missing in 1845. Following a period of searching for Franklin, Pacaud returned to Ottawa, Ontario, where he began to serve in the war of 1812 as a navigator for the Marine Department. Later, he became an aide to Franklin's successor, Rear Admiral Henry Kellet.

After the war, he settled in Quebec and became an active volunteer with the Congrégation de Notre-Dame, a religious order of Catholic nuns, before his death in 1885.

Lucien Turcotte Pacaud (7 October 1877 – 18 February 1955) was a French-Canadian businessman and politician. He served in the Legislative Assembly of Quebec from 1924 to 1936 as a Liberal. He was one of the leading figures in the industrial and economic development of the Gaspe region.

Pacaud was born in Tracadie-Sheila, New Brunswick, the son of Pascal Babin Pacaud and Clélie Lebel. He obtained his early education in Tracadie and in New Carlisle, Quebec. Lucien Turcotte Pacaud (2 February 1889 – 51 August 1947) was a Canadian pioneer in the field of architecture, focusing heavily on education and public health. He designed some of the earliest modern school buildings in the province of Quebec. He is considered the founder of the modern school movement in the province. 

Pacaud was born in Cap-de-la-Madeleine near Trois-Rivières. In 1915 he graduated from Laval University with a diploma in architecture. Lucien Turcotte-Pacaud (1894–1979) was an author and professor of French literature at Université Laval in Quebec. He is best known for his works on traditional French-Canadian literature and culture in the province of Quebec, particularly in the fields of French-Canadian poetry and folklore. A strong proponent of Quebec's cultural nationalism and resistance to assimilation, he is also remembered for his contributions to Quebec's cultural heritage.

Turcotte-Pacaud sought to authoritatively document France-Canadian literature, while also advocating for its continued influence in the province. He published several landmark works on Quebec literature, including Littérature canadienne-française (1970), concerning the development of French-Canadian poetry since its inception until 1970; Poésies folkloriques des Canadiens-français (1972), a major anthology collection of traditional French-Canadian poetry; and La littérature canadienne-française, une histoire d’amour (1979), which surveyed the history of French-Canadian fiction from its beginnings to 1979. Lucien Turcotte Pacaud (1882 – 1949) was a Canadian civil servant and public servant from Quebec, who served in many roles throughout his career. He is best known for being the Inspector General of the International Joint Commission from 1920–1935 and as civil secretary for Quebec from 1936–1949.

In 1907, Pacaud was appointed as an auditor with the Department of Public Works Canada and became one of the most influential civil servants at the time. He was appointed to the Board of Control of Canada in 1915, where he served until 1920 when he was appointed to the International Joint Commission (IJC). The IJC was responsible for resolving disputes between the United States and Canada, and Pacaud thus embodied important diplomatic roles. He served as inspector general of the IJC from 1920 to 1935 and negotiated a number of significant international treaties and agreements.

Lucien Turcotte Pacaud (19 June 1888 – 2 April 1974) was a Canadian tenor, composer and Professor of Music at the Université de Montréal. Pacaud was born in Montreal and went on to become one of Canada's best-known and beloved tenors. As a passionate supporter of contemporary Quebec music, he gave numerous performances throughout his life and was often accompanied by his wife, pianist Léontine Lebel Pacaud. He also established a presence in the international classical music world, performing solo works for the French composer Claude Debussy, the Irish composer Elizabeth Maconchy, the English composer Benjamin Britten, the German composer Kurt Weill, among others. He was well-known for his renditions of the works of Québécois composers such as Clermont Pépin, Gilles Tremblay, and Jean Papineau-Couture. He was also celebrated for his interpretations of works by Italian, German, French and British composers. Lucien Turcotte Pacaud (1873–1956) was a French Canadian lawyer and senator. He was born on October 3, 1873 in Quebec City, Quebec.

Pacaud was educated at the Université Laval (law degree, 1897), becoming an advocate at the Quebec Bar in 1897. He excelled in his career and became a judge of the Superior Court of the Province of Quebec in 1913.

He was appointed to the Senate of Canada for the division of Autray, Quebec in 1926. In the Senate, Pacaud was particularly active as a member of the Banking and Commerce Committee, where he played a major role in the passage of the bill that established the Bank of Canada.

Pacaud was also a strong advocate for the advancement of French-Canadian interests and promote the French language in Canada. He was a dedicated public servant who held the respect of all his political adversaries, and he was widely respected for his legal expertise.

Pacaud died in his native Quebec City, on June 24, 1956.

Lucien Turcotte Pacaud (July 4, 1894 - February 25, 1964) was a Canadian lawyer, civil servant and political figure. He represented Beauce in the Legislative Assembly of Quebec from 1928 to 1944 as a Conservative.

He was born in Saint-Gédéon-de-Beauce, Quebec, the son of Louison Turcotte and Dalia Pacaud. He was educated there and at Université Laval. Turcotte Pacaud was called to the Quebec bar in 1916 and set up practice in Montreal. In 1918, he married Marie-Eugénie Gauthier. He remained in the Quebec assembly until 1945. Turcotte Pacaud was defeated in the provincial seat in 1944.

sentences: Kate Deines (born April 15, 1988) is an American professional soccer player who currently plays for Reign FC of the National Women's Soccer League (NWSL).
She previously played for the Seattle Sounders Women of the W-League, the Washington Spirit of the NWSL, and the Seattle Reign FC of the NWSL.
Deines was born in Seattle, Washington and attended Newport High School in Bellevue, Washington.
She played college soccer at the University of Washington from 2006 to 2009.
During her college career, she was named to the All-Pac-10 First Team in 2008 and 2009.
Deines was drafted by the Washington Freedom in the 2010 WPS Draft.
She made her professional debut on April 11, 2010, in a match against the Atlanta Beat.
She played for the Freedom until the team folded in 2011.
In 2012, Deines signed with the Seattle Sounders Women of the W-League.

In the 1930s, Loder moved to the Decca label, where he worked as a sound engineer and producer.
He was responsible for the production of some of the most successful British recordings of the era, including those of Vera Lynn, Gracie Fields, and George Formby.
Loder continued to work in the music industry until his death in 1972.
He was posthumously inducted into the Music Producers Guild Hall of Fame in 2008.

sentences: A. P. J. Abdul Kalam (15 October 1931 – 27 July 2015) was an Indian scientist and politician who served as the 11th President of India from 2002 to 2007.
He was born and raised in Rameswaram, Tamil Nadu and studied physics and aerospace engineering.

He spent the next four decades as a scientist and science administrator, mainly at the Defence Research and Development Organisation (DRDO) and Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) and was intimately involved in India's civilian space programme and military missile development efforts.
He thus came to be known as the Missile Man of India for his work on the development of ballistic missile and launch vehicle technology.
He also played a pivotal organisational, technical, and political role in India's Pokhran-II nuclear tests in 1998, the first since the original nuclear test by India in 1974.

He also wrote for The New Masses, a Communist magazine, and was a founding member of the American Writers' Congress.
Reed was arrested in 1923 for his involvement in the Communist Party and served a year in prison.
After his release, he continued to write and speak about Communism and the Soviet Union, and he was a delegate to the Seventh World Congress of the Communist International in Moscow in 1935.
He died in Moscow in 1980.

sentences: Vitaliano Brancati (1907–1954) was an Italian novelist, playwright, screenwriter, and journalist.
He was born in Pachino, Sicily, and studied law at the University of Catania.
He was a major figure in the Italian literary scene of the 1940s and 1950s, and is considered one of the most important Italian writers of the twentieth century.
Brancati's works are characterized by a combination of realism and fantasy, often with a satirical edge.
His most famous novel is Don Camillo e l'onorevole
Peppone (1947), which was adapted into a popular film series starring Fernandel and Gino Cervi.
Other notable works include Il bell'Antonio (1950), La provinciale (1951), and Il diavolo al Pontelungo (1953).
He also wrote several plays and screenplays, including the script for the film La terra trema (1948).
Brancati died in Rome in 1954.

He ascended to the throne of the Northern Qi in 626 after the death of his father, Emperor Gao Zhen. He was considered a good ruler and was popularly known as "the Eternal Spring Emperor". He was successful in restoring the economy of the Northern Qi as well as preserving its security, and was known for his tolerance of Buddhists and Daoists. He also created an effective system of government, in which imperial advisors (quanjun) were drawn from the civil service.

Emperor Wenxuan declared himself the Heavenly King and initiated a reign of terror over his court, executing some of his most trusted and loyal ministers. He also killed his opponents in gruesome ways, such as removing their teeth and cutting off their tongues. Despite these actions, he is remembered by many Chinese as a great emperor and a generous ruler. He also appointed many officials and administrators from among honest and capable people, regardless of their relatively lower social status. He was credited for restoring peace and order to Northern Qi, even though his reign was short-lived.

Stereophonic sound or, more commonly, stereo, is a method of sound reproduction that creates an illusion of multi-directional audible perspective. This is typically achieved by using two or more independent audio channels through a configuration of two or more loudspeakers (or stereo headphones) in such a way as to create the impression of sound heard from various directions, as in natural hearing. Thus the term "stereophonic" applies to so-called "quadraphonic" and "surround-sound" systems as well as the more common two-channel, two-speaker systems. It is often contrasted with monophonic, or "mono" sound, where audio is in the form of one channel, often centered in the sound field.

Swanson served on numerous government and scientific committees, including the Air Force Scientific Advisory Board, Office of Naval Research, National Science Foundation, and the Department of Health Education and Welfare. He was a founding member of the Institute for Mathematical Statistics. Swanson was a corresponding member of the National Academy of Sciences and was awarded the Florence Nightingale David Award in 1973. He received honors from the American Statistical Association, American Mathematical Society and Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers.

Don R. Swanson (December 28, 1929 – August 8, 2017) was an American engineer. He was best known for his work at the Boeing Company in engineering, and he led the redesign effort of the wing on the 757 which turned it into one of Boeing's most successful aircraft. He was also the lead engineer on the 737 and a key figure in the introduction of the 777. Swanson was born in Kansas in 1929. He attended the University of Kansas, where he earned degrees in both aeronautical and mechanical engineering. After graduating in 1956, he joined Boeing and soon became head of their new Advanced Projects division. He was credited with helping to get the 757 and 737 off the drawing board and into the air. He later worked on the 777, which was revolutionary in its use of composite materials and its fuel efficient design. Swanson retired from Boeing in 1994 after 37 years of service. He was recognized for his contributions to the aerospace industry with numerous awards, including the Mark J. Mellett award for Outstanding Leadership from the American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics, two National Aerospace Awards from the National Aeronautic

Don R. Swanson (April 1, 1921 – October 13, 2000) was an American educator and the first President of the University of North Dakota (UND) in Grand Forks. A native of Westhope, North Dakota, Swanson earned a B.A. degree from UND, a master's degree in education from the University of Minnesota, and a doctorate in education from the University of Illinois. Swanson served as president of UND from 1965–1984. During his two-decade tenure, he guided the university's growth from 4,000 to 17,000 students and its addition of numerous new academic and support programs. He is credited with helping to establish UND as an American center of excellence in aviation, engineering, and geology.

Don R. Swanson (1941–2015) was an American physician and medical researcher who made major contributions to the specialty of radiologic pathology. He was a professor of pathology at the University of Minnesota and Medical School, as well as a pioneer in developing a system for the interpretation of medical images for diagnosis. He was the first to articulate the concept of the "Three Rs" – Receptive, Reliable, and Rigorous – which has been used as a framework for teaching, research and practice in radiologic pathology. He was also a prolific author, contributing to numerous publications, including the Handbook of Radiologic Pathology, the Encyclopedia of Radiologic Pathology, and Ultrasound and Other Advanced Technologies in Radiological Pathology, among others. He was a member of several professional organizations, including Phi Beta Kappa, the American Radiological Society and the American College of Radiology Imaging Network. His pioneering work has been recognized by a number of medical and academic awards, including the Thomas Edison Medal, the American Cancer Society's Ray E Study Award, and the Lifetime Achievement Award for Radiologic Pathology from the UCLA Center for Health Sciences.

Don Robert Swanson (born May 15, 1926) was an American military veteran, astronaut, and former test pilot.

Swanson was born in Beaverdam, Ohio. After graduating from high school in 1940, he attended Ohio State University, where he earned a bachelor's degree in Mechanical Engineering in 1950. He then joined the United States Air Force and became a fighter pilot, achieving the rank of Lieutenant Colonel in the Air Force Reserve. During his military service he flew 100 missions in Vietnam and was the first military reservations to be assigned to the U.S. Space Command in 1987. Following his retirement from the military, Swanson became an engineer at NASA's Johnson Space Center in Texas, where he worked in astronaut training and supervising the development of the Space Shuttle program.

From 1994-1999, Swanson served as a NASA astronaut, flying two missions aboard the Space Shuttle Columbia and becoming the first U.S. military veteran to travel into space. On his first mission, he completed 140 orbits of the earth, while on his second mission he completed 215 orbits of the earth and led three spacewalks. Don R. Swanson (born 1950) is a Professor of Library and Information Science at the University of Illinois at Urbana–Champaign and director of the Graduate School of Library and Information Science. He is one of the international leaders in the field of information science.

Swanson is particularly notable for the Developmental Model of Information Seeking (DMIS) and for his research on information seeking and retrieval, user studies and information behavior. Swanson has also been a strong advocate of the integration of library and information science with computer science, which he demonstrated in his 2006 book The Kind of Motion We Call Heat: Sources and Studies in the History of Information Science written with his colleague and former UIUC colleague Yoram Ankori.

He has received numerous awards and honors, including: award of merit from the Association for Library and Information Science Education in 2011, the ASIST research award in 2010, the ASIST Jakob Nielsen Award in 2007, and the ACM SIGIR Salton Award in 2005. He is a past president of the American Society for Information Science and Technology and an ASIS&T fellow. He was elected to the American Academy of

Donald R. Swanson (May 24, 1916 – October 25, 1991) was an American chemist who developed artificial flavorings and preservatives for the food industry. He was born in Chicago, Illinois. 

Swanson attended the University of Chicago, where he earned a B.S. in Chemistry in 1937. He then joined the research staff of the Flavor Division of Swift & Company. After World War II, he became director of the Flavor Division's research facility in Philadelphia. From 1951 to 1952, Swanson was a visiting professor of food chemistry at Michigan State University.

In 1972, Swanson was appointed vice president of research and development for the Food Technology Division of International Flavors and Fragrances. He retired from that position in 1987. Throughout his career, Swanson published over 100 articles in technical magazines and several books about the science of flavor chemistry. After his retirement, he was a consultant to the flavor industry until 1990 and a tireless advocate for food safety.

Don R. Swanson (born October 27, 1927) is an American professor and researcher, widely acknowledged as the father of information science. Swanson is the author of several hundreds of publications and awarded numerous awards from national and international governments and societies.

Swanson received his B.S. degree in Physics from Queens' College of the University of Cambridge in 1950, his M.S. degree in Mathematics from Harvard University in 1952 and his Ph.D. degree in Mathematics and Mechanics from Harvard University in 1956. He joined the faculty of the University of Chicago in 1956 as Professor of Library Science and held the endowed Frohman Chair of Library Science from 1966 until his retirement in 1996.

Throughout his academic life, Swanson sought to develop multidisciplinary theories linking the sciences, humanities, mathematics, and library science. He furthered collaboration across disciplines that still affects the field today. In addition, Swanson developed models and theories to facilitate the retrieval of information. His groundbreaking breakthroughs in theories such as co-word analysis, spectrum analysis, and content analysis formed the foundations for the study of information science.

Don R. Swanson (March 10, 1925 – May 23, 1988) was a theorist in the fields of library science and information science. His research focused on software applications, information retrieval systems and library automations. He was a professor at the University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA) and a professor emeritus at the University of California, San Diego (UCSD).

Swanson is widely recognized for his contributions to information science. He wrote extensively on the role and application of information retrieval systems. His 1968 essay "Information Needs and Uses" is widely considered to be seminal work on the subject. He was also instrumental in developing MARC records as a method of indexing bibliographic information and creating a framework for libraries to share resources.

In addition to his academic work, Swanson was a consultant for several library organizations including the American Library Association, IBM, and the Library of Congress. He was also a founding member of the American Society for Information Science and the Institute for Scientific Information. Don R. Swanson (1915–1970) was a United States Navy lieutenant and an aviation pioneer. He was awarded the Distinguished Flying Cross for his bravery while piloting a Grumman F6F Hellcat in the Pacific during World War II.

In addition to his heroism during the war, Swanson was also a renowned aeronautical innovator. In 1949, he developed a one-man helicopter that would revolutionize the aviation industry. The first prototype of the Swanson Helicopter, built upon the design of the Johnson Rocket, first flew on April 7, 1949. This was the first helicopter that could fly with only one person, making it much easier and more practical than earlier two- and three-person designs.

Swanson founded the American Helicopter and Aeronautical Corporation in 1950 and became the company’s first president and general manager. Under Swanson’s leadership, the company developed a series of groundbreaking helicopters, including the first helicopter with automatic control systems, the AH-1, and the first mass-produced helicopter for the US Military, the AH-2 Seasprite.

Don R. Swanson (1931–1995) was an American librarian, library science and information science scholar, author, and educator. He was the author of more than 200 publications and a professor emeritus at the University of Pittsburgh, where he taught and researched for more than 30 years.

Swanson was one of the leading scholars in the fields of library science and information science, with a particular emphasis on citation analysis, subject indexing, and the relationships between library users, librarians, and information. He was a major proponent of user-centered design, arguing that libraries should be designed from the perspective of the information user rather than from the perspective of the librarian. He was also a strong advocate for interdisciplinary approaches to the study and analysis of information.

Don R. Swanson (1931 - 2020) was an American physicist and inventor. He was a professor of mathematics and physics at Southern Illinois University, as well as a senior staff scientist with Argonne National Laboratory. Swanson is best known for his invention of the double-stage light pipe in the early 1970s, which revolutionized the field of nuclear medicine.

Swanson was born in 1931 in St. Louis, Missouri. He received his bachelor's degree in physics from Washington University in St. Louis in 1953 and obtained his doctorate in physics from Northwestern University in 1958. Following graduation, he worked as a physicist at Argonne National Laboratory.

Swanson performed research at Argonne in nuclear medicine and imaging, nuclear reactor physics, and cold fusion. He was responsible for numerous patents, papers, and technical reports in the field of nuclear physics. Don R. Swanson (May 28, 1920 – April 11, 2002) was an American engineer, inventor and entrepreneur. He was born in Mexico, Indiana and grew up in a poverty-stricken family in Omaha, Nebraska. Swanson attended Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute (RPI) and received a degree in Civil Engineering in 1941. He worked for an engineering firm in Washington D.C. before being drafted by the Army in 1942.

Swanson was an innovative engineer who received 14 patents for inventions, primarily related to mobile electric power and the telecommunications industry. He founded several telecommunications, construction, and engineering companies, all stemming from his pioneering ideas. Most notably, he founded Swanson Technology, which provided consulting services, engineering and project management in the telecommunications engineering industry. He was a founding member of the Society of Telecommunications Engineers and received numerous awards for his professional accomplishments.

Swanson was also deeply committed to giving to the community. He was a life-long advocate for education, believing that children are often limited by a lack of resources. He was an active supporter of the local school system, creating scholarships and providing tutoring and

Don R. Swanson (April 16, 1931 – May 5, 1981) was an American physicist and author, best remembered for his pioneering work on diffusion-controlled reactions. Born in LaGrande, Oregon, he attended Oregon State University, and received his PhD in physics from the University of Wisconsin in 1961.

Swanson was a faculty member at the University of Michigan from 1961 to 1967, and then taught at the University of Chicago until 1981. At the University of Chicago, he wrote and published papers establishing the foundations of reaction-diffusion theory and, along with A. McKane and U. Ebert, developed a mathematical model for the reaction-diffusion waves observed in chemical solutions and biological systems. His contribution to mathematics earned him the 1974 George Ledlie Prize from the National Academy of Sciences.

Swanson died of a brain tumor in 1981 at the age of 50, leaving behind a legacy as one of the most influential physicists of the 20th century.

Don R. Swanson (July 1, 1910 – April 5, 1991) was an American librarian and library scholar, who served as dean of library administration at the University of Illinois from 1946 until his retirement in September 1974. Through his library system, he was influential in the development of library science in the United States.

Swanson was a leading pioneer in the development of library science and the application of information technology to library services. He was an advocate of interlibrary cooperation, belief in universal access to information, and in service to the underserved. His development of the Dewey Decimal System, the Library of Congress Classification System, and his implementation of the MARC (Machine Readable Cataloging) system played a large role in organizing library resources and improving access to them. He was involved in library automation and in the establishment of electronic information networks.

Swanson was also a major figure in the professionalization of librarianship. He was the first president of the American Library Association and the Library Administration and Management Association. Don R. Swanson (April 14, 1933 – May 7, 1999) was an American library scientist and educator. He was a pioneering force in the fields of library science, information management and online information retrieval, foremost in the area of chemical information retrieval. He published more than 140 articles and books, and was a Fellow of the American Association for the Advancement of Science (AAAS).

Swanson was born in Homestead, Pennsylvania, and obtained his bachelor's degree in chemistry cum laude from the University of Pittsburgh in 1954. He subsequently earned a master's degree in organic chemistry at the Carnegie Institute of Technology 1957, and a master's degree in library science from the same institution in 1958.

Swanson went on to be a faculty member at the University of Pittsburgh School of Library and Information Sciences from 1959 until 1997, where he taught courses in advanced chemical information systems, computer-based information retrieval, and information organization. He was a prominent figure in the development of computer-based information retrieval systems for scientific and technical information. His interests ranged from traditional information systems and classification schemes to fuzzy logic and artificial intelligence. After his retirement in 1997,

Don R. Swanson (1926–2002) was an American computer scientist and professor at the University of Chicago. He was a pioneer in information science, particularly in the areas of knowledge organization, retrieval, and synthesis. He developed the concept of “relatedness”, which views entities as existing in relation to each other. He created the phrase “precision and recall” for information retrieval systems.

Swanson developed the precursor to today's thesauri, called "Inverse Document Frequency" (IDF), and worked on the development of the first automatic indexing system, the SMART system. He is credited with having created the "Keyword in Context" indexing method and for his early work on probabilistic retrieval models. He worked with the Institute for Scientific Information in creating the Science Citation Index and its derivatives. He was also a co-founder and past president of the American Society for Information Science and Technology.

Don R. Swanson (April 8, 1925 – September 4, 1975) was an American music educator, conductor, clinician and professor. He is best remembered for the creation of nine choral publications for his church, school and studio choirs, which have become widely recognized and used in the United States and around the world.

Swanson was born in Oswego, Illinois and began his music education at Oberlin College and Conservatory, where he earned both a Bachelor and a Master of Music degree. During his seven-year tenure as Professor of Music at Central Methodist College, Swanson pioneered many developments in choral music throughout the Midwest. As founder and choirmaster of the Kansas City Dinner Choir, he is credited with introducing collegiate-level standard of choral performance in the mid-Illinois region. 

He is also credited with the founding of the Wisconsin All-State Chorus, the MacMurray Music Institute, and the National Choral Festival of Wisconsin, as well as the programming of numerous choral works in the Midwest.
